title_en: What I Learned from Running Events for a Month
excerpt_en: So, it's Saturday. And so, I held a walking event in the morning! The turnout was worse than ever, and only 5 people showed up this time. 10 had registered, so that's half. That said, it's not a problem when it comes to actually running
body_en:
So, it's Saturday.

And so,

I held a walking event in the morning!

The turnout was worse than ever,

and only 5 people showed up this time.

10 had registered, so that's half.

That said,

it's not a problem when it comes to actually running the event.

With fewer people,

we can walk around the city center.

Including me, there were 6 of us,

so walking for about an hour

meant I could interact with everyone.

In fact, this time,

I got to talk with each person for about 10 minutes!

We had a diverse group from Germany, Italy, Poland, China, and Japan.

It only works because people show up on weekends,

so I'm nothing but grateful

to everyone who comes.

I want to keep this weekly walking event going

for as long as I'm in Ireland.

The Purpose of the Events

The purpose of holding events is

to get as many people as possible

to know about the FOMUS brand.

To do that, rather than the brand itself,

people need to get to know "MaSU" first --

the person running it.

I think it's still a bit early

for the brand to stand on its own.

Events can't be run as pure volunteer work.

I'm paying 25,000 yen a year as an initial investment

just to create a group on the app,

so I need to generate some revenue too.

Right now,

the revenue from events is tiny,

but to spread FOMUS,

instead of paying money to go meet people,

I'm actually getting paid

while people join the events,

which makes me really happy.

That said, if the numbers drop,

running events becomes difficult.

For example, tomorrow, Sunday,

there are only 2 reservations.

So I cancelled it.

What I learned from running events this past month

is that Sundays are harder to gather people.

They probably just want to rest properly, I think.

Also, at most we had 20 people,

and at least 5 showed up.

Regarding the turnout,

the advertising cost is zero,

and everything is done within the app.

That part is a good situation.

However, the numbers are also unstable.

Personally,

the ideal situation would be for a month's events

to cover my rent.

The cheapest rent is 120,000 yen,

so that's about 30,000 yen per week.

In other words, if 40 people consistently show up

to my weekly events, I clear this game.

And from here on, it's going to get colder and colder.

I feel like the hurdle of waking up early

and walking is going to get even higher.

So from here,

I need to gradually introduce changes.

Changing the time, changing the content --

there's lots to do.

For now, starting in October,

I changed the Sunday walking event

from 9 AM to 10 AM.

To allow people to start

a little more leisurely.

Also, it's not just the walking events --

the card game events

also need a change in approach soon.

First of all, the card game events

have almost no repeat visitors.

It's always new people showing up.

That's impressive in its own way,

but personally, I'd like to see more repeat participants joining gradually.

In other words,

the connection through "MaSU" himself, beyond the card game,

is probably weak.

While walking events have plenty of time for real interaction, card game events don't allow as much time to get to know each other.

The card game is just a means,

and the goal is interaction and connection,

but I'm probably not doing that part well enough.

Since I'm running both events simultaneously, I can compare them, so it's important to try various things and collect data.

Also, I need to build a system

where 20 people consistently show up

every time.

This is exactly what fan-building is about,

and I also need people to bring their friends along.

Building repeat visitors who come "because MaSU is hosting it,"

while also attracting new users who "want to join a popular event" -- that's an absolute requirement.

This is something I've never done

in Japan either,

so it's worth the challenge.

If I can pull this off overseas,

Japan will be much easier,

so I'm going to build this skill while I'm still in Ireland.

title_en: The Challenges Are Piling Up, but I Just Have to Do It
excerpt_en: Today is Sunday! Normally I'd have a walking event in the morning, but since reservations were under 5, I cancelled it in advance. The initial organizer bonus on the Meetup app has probably ended. From here on is where I really show what I'm made of.
body_en:
Today is Sunday!

Normally I'd have a walking event in the morning,

but since reservations were under 5, I cancelled it in advance.

The initial organizer bonus on the Meetup app

has probably

ended.

From here on is where I really show what I'm made of!

So,

I had no plans in the morning,

so I spent the whole morning grinding through work,

and in the afternoon, I attended

the Japan meetup for the first time in a while.

The people here are Japanese students

and foreigners who like Japan.

Or older guys who like Japanese people.

Honestly, unlike the first month

when I needed to gather info about Dublin,

when it comes to other topics,

there isn't much that's personally useful.

There's the benefit of creating opportunities to speak English

and the benefit of expanding my network,

but it's not like any business connections

come out of it here.

In that case,

the Wednesday meetup is definitely better.

Furthermore, after this event,

I had my own card game event.

Today there were 7 reservations,

but only 3 actually showed up.

And on top of that,

one woman left before we even started playing cards.

She was saying, "Wait, this is all the people there are?"

But you can see the number of participants when you reserve,

and it's a mistake to assume everyone will actually show up.

I've felt that way at other events too, and if her goal was something like "meeting men and women," then this card game event isn't the right fit. Haha.

Since it could lower the satisfaction level,

it was actually better that she left before we started,

but I thought, "She could've at least said something before leaving."

*She asked where the restroom was, and then just disappeared.

The card game event venue was

the same venue as the Japan meetup.

But this time, quite a few people from the Japan meetup wandered over to the card game event side,

and we couldn't

concentrate on playing

the card game "SILVA."

Especially since there was another Japanese person besides me,

some people mistakenly came thinking it was the Japan meetup.

That's not what I'm going for,

so starting next time, I'll change the venue

to the hotel lounge, same as Wednesdays,

to deal with this.

Ideally, I'd like to continue right after the Japan meetup event,

but with interruptions like this

and difficulty securing seats,

I've come to realize it's tricky in various ways,

which led to this decision.

That said, the Japan meetup and

the next card game venue are a 3-minute walk apart,

so honestly, it's not that big of a deal.

For now, I'll keep trying different things and find the best setup.

Toward the end of the event,

someone from the Japan meetup

came over, and that person was

one of those rare "people I don't want to talk to."

He's Irish,

and while he can speak Japanese to some extent,

he has "communication issues,"

and talking to him is just painfully boring.

And I can't even bring myself to want to dig deeper into who he is, and I instinctively feel like spending time on that is a waste of life.

You don't come across people like that often,

but every once in a while, you do.

Honestly, the moment this guy barged in,

I wanted to exclude him (to protect the group), but since the other participant didn't know him, they said "sure, sit down,"

and at that point, my motivation to play card games dropped to zero.

*Once you've let them in, they might spread a bad impression to others.

We'd already been going for about an hour and a half,

so I ended the event.

That other participant must have realized it quickly after talking to the Irish guy.

They left at the same time as us. Haha.

To prevent this kind of disruption,

I definitely shouldn't use the same venue.

Since the event ended on an unsatisfying note,

I headed straight to a pub with

a Japanese friend who came to the card game event for the first time.

I was starving,

so I ordered a hamburger.

We chatted about all sorts of things.

And just like that,

Week 9 flew by in no time.

This week,

between moving and catching a cold,

it was a tough week all around,

but I managed to get through it.

I'm thinking it's about time

to start shooting for Masu Photo again too.

The challenges are piling up,

but I want to tackle them one by one.

excerpt_en: After school, I went to the Asian market. As the name suggests, it's a shop that sells Asian food. Mainly Korean, Chinese, and Japanese ingredients. They sell soy sauce, mirin, udon, curry, and more. Right now, I'm living in a shared house, and
body_en:
After school, I went to the Asian market.

As the name suggests,

it's a shop that sells Asian food.

Mainly Korean, Chinese, and Japanese ingredients.

They sell soy sauce, mirin, udon, curry, and more.

Right now, I'm living in a shared house,

and even if I buy a lot of groceries, the fridge space available to me isn't very big,

and even if I cook,

I don't have containers to pack food in,

so there are various difficulties.

It's been 2 months since I came to Ireland.

3 months since I left Japan,

so I've been missing Japanese food,

and what I bought at the Asian market was

"natto"!

You just end up craving it.

Besides that, there were Kumamoto and Fukuoka cup noodles manufactured in a Hong Kong factory that Nissin licensed, so I bought those too.

Also, I suddenly craved Shin Ramyun for the first time in a while,

so I bought that too.

For the first time in a while,

I bought some junk food.

*I probably won't buy any for a while.

What I noticed from visiting the Asian market

is that about 80% of the customers are Asian.

There were some Europeans too.

For Asian people living locally,

it must be a precious shop.

They didn't sell Japanese microwaveable rice,

but they had Korean microwaveable rice, so I bought it!

It's not as good as Japanese ones,

but better than nothing!

I was able to have natto with it!

*The natto itself wasn't that good though.

While having that experience,

after lunch, I buried myself in work!

excerpt_en: So, October has arrived. Only 3 months left in 2024! Time really flies by so fast, it's scary. I'm now in Week 10 of language school. In just 3 more weeks, I'll be graduating from school. As I wrote a little while ago,
body_en:
So, October has arrived.

Only 3 months left in 2024!

Time really flies by so fast,

it's scary.

I'm now in Week 10 of language school.

In just 3 more weeks, I'll be graduating from school.

As I wrote a little while ago,

I'm currently in the intermediate class.

Honestly, the students' levels aren't that different, but the teacher's speaking speed has gone up dramatically.

My listening skills are still weak,

and there are times when I can't understand what the teacher is saying.

Even so, I try to keep up,

and I start thinking about what the teacher is trying to say,

so my ability to guess improves.

But this doesn't mean

I'm actually building English skills.

I've also been doing the international exchange events for a month,

and there are still words I don't understand sometimes.

I really want to be able to speak properly,

and I have a strong desire

to take my comprehension of what others are saying

from 70% to 100%.

That's why I study English every day,

but the reality is it's still not enough.

Moving to an English-speaking environment

was the right call for me,

but honestly, I need to immerse myself in more English,

and if I don't know the vocabulary in the first place,

even if I hear it, I won't know what it means,

so what I need to do is vocabulary.

I'm stunned by my own lack of ability

and how far I still have to go,

but it's my own fault for not putting in the work until now,

so I'll keep at it, steadily, without getting discouraged.

*A view of Dublin

excerpt_en: It's Wednesday, so it's time for the regular card game international exchange event! As I've written many times, it's an event where we play and have fun using "SILVA," a card game developed by FOMUS, while doing international exchange. This time, 10 people participated
body_en:
It's Wednesday,

so it's time for the regular card game international exchange event!

As I've written many times,

it's an event where we play and have fun using "SILVA," a card game developed by FOMUS, while doing international exchange.

This time, 10 people participated!

This might be the highest turnout ever!

Weekday evenings are definitely a good call!

#The numbers fluctuate but demand outside of weekends is confirmed

I had shared the rules within the Meetup app again this time, and someone actually studied them beforehand!

They even watched the YouTube video!

Since I've only made a Japanese version on YouTube,

foreigners can barely understand it,

so the fact that they studied beforehand

makes me really happy.

I've gotten more used to explaining the rules

through repetition,

and I've also started thinking about

how to get communication going

before starting the card game.

Up until now, I was starting the card game early on, but since this is an international exchange event,

I also make sure to set aside time for everyone to interact.

That said, it's only about 20 minutes,

so the key is how to make it meaningful.

Through trial and error,

figuring out how to increase satisfaction

is my role.

It was scheduled from 6 PM to 7:30 PM,

but SILVA got so heated

that we ended up having fun until around 8:30 PM!

Having people from various countries come together

and enjoy a card game made by FOMUS

and get excited about it is truly rewarding,

and the people I meet here aren't just one-time encounters --

someday, we might connect again through SILVA, walking,

or something completely different.

It's been 10 weeks since I came to Dublin.

Building an event from scratch

and creating a space like this

is personally super interesting.

Also, I'd been thinking about going to Bulgaria soon, and someone from Bulgaria showed up at this event and taught me all sorts of things.

This kind of thing might be

one of the best parts about hosting events!

Wednesdays Also Have the Japan Meetup!

After the card game event ended,

I walked about 3 minutes to the Japan meetup.

This is a place where, every Wednesday,

Japanese people and people interested in Japan gather.

There are always new people,

as well as people who've been coming for years.

It's interesting to meet all sorts of people.

Since I show up at events

and also host my own events,

for only having been in Dublin for 2 months,

my face is becoming well known.

This is something to be grateful for,

but it also raises the difficulty level.

Other people know me,

and I'm the only one who doesn't know them,

which happens more and more,

and that's tough too. Haha.

So, as my circle of acquaintances has been growing,

at some point I ended up drinking with a group of people I'd met two weeks earlier who've been active in Dublin for a long time.

My time in Dublin is by far the shortest,

but since my FOMUS activities,

walking events, card game events, and such

are all visible activities I'm doing in Dublin,

I have more talking points than the average person,

and we get along well.

In business and outside of business,

it all comes down to "people" in the end,

so first, I need people to find "MaSU" interesting,

and getting them to think "I want to do something with this guy!" is the top priority.

This has always been the case since I was in Japan --

it's more important to sell myself first than the content.

Of course, the other person is the same --

they look at the "person" running it.

This is incredibly important!

You don't become close friends right away from one meeting,

so I keep meeting people over and over to close the distance.

To do that,

I need to make my project here even bigger, so the challenges are piling up! Haha.

And so, every Wednesday, I've got

two events back to back.

This week was another incredibly stimulating day.

FOMUS spread a little more again,

so I'll keep expanding it steadily!

excerpt_en: After school, I went to a print shop. The purpose was to create materials for hosting an art exhibition here. Back in May this year, I had the experience of hosting a FOMUS-organized art exhibition in Japan, and I have the desire to hold an art
body_en:
After school, I went to a print shop.

The purpose was to create materials for hosting an art exhibition here.

Back in May this year, in Japan,

I had the experience of hosting a FOMUS-organized art exhibition,

and I have the desire to hold an art exhibition in Dublin at some point too.

Because some people get interested in "masu" through art.

To do that, I need materials.

I have data from the art exhibition I held in Japan,

but those are just photos from the event itself --

it's pointless if I can't show the actual artworks.

Then, I'll just make them here!

And so,

I had purchased picture frames a few weeks ago,

and today I went to a slightly upscale print shop

to get the designs printed.

Honestly, if it were just regular printing,

there are cheap print shops around,

so I could just print there,

but since this is also about creating materials,

skimping on the cost is not an option.

Plus, square-format printing

can't be done on a regular copier,

so leaving it to the pros is the best choice.

And so,

I brought the picture frames I'd bought earlier,

and asked the staff,

"I want to display artwork in these frames -- how should I go about it?"

I asked directly and they taught me.

Things work differently from Japan,

so this kind of experience is interesting too.

This time it was just 2 prints,

but the price was higher than expected. Haha.

I think these artworks will be

put up for sale at some point too,

but I want to make sure they have proper scarcity value

and deliver them to people who really want them.

Apparently you can order prints online,

and now that I know the full ordering process,

next time I need to print, I only need to come

to pick up the finished prints!

And the pieces I'm printing this time

should be done by tomorrow.

Actually, next Wednesday,

I've been invited to a somewhat formal

Japanese-Irish event,

and at that opportunity, I'm hoping to

promote myself along with

the masu and the art pieces.

And so, I'm doing everything I can right now

to spread FOMUS

from every possible angle.

A Run for the First Time in a While

Since I got home a bit early

and my work was getting stale,

I went for a run around my neighborhood for a change of pace.

My current place is in Cabra,

and there's a massive park nearby.

Its name is Phoenix Park!

Language school students apparently go hang out there with friends often, but I'm here for a solo run!

Along the way, I spotted the famous deer.

The paths are well-maintained,

making it a perfect running course!

I finished about 10 km in around an hour.

I hadn't been able to do a serious run lately,

so it had been a while,

but my body can handle 10 km relatively easily.

Among all the interesting people I've met in my life, every single one of them has been a stamina monster, and from my experience, I know that "physical endurance" is incredibly important for doing anything.

Since your stamina starts to gradually decline in your 30s, I want to train properly and build up my base fitness.

And I'll develop the stamina and mental toughness

to do even more exciting things!

excerpt_en: After school, I headed to yesterday's print shop. I'd already received an email saying the printing was done, so I went to pick it up right away. Picking it up was super easy -- I just told them my reservation number and they brought it right out. And then,
body_en:
After school,

I headed to yesterday's print shop.

I'd already received an email saying the printing was done, so I went to pick it up right away.

Picking it up was super easy --

I just told them my reservation number and they brought it right out.

And then, time to frame them!

They turned out quite nicely!

I plan to unveil these at the Japanese-Irish embassy-related event on October 9th.

If these art pieces sell,

I can earn my activity expenses in Ireland from them, so from here on, how I market them is where my skills come in!

That said, I still haven't found a concrete method, but for now, I'll just go for it!

After that, it was all work!

That said, aside from language school,

all I do is work,

so it's business as usual.

Between event prep and other things,

the day flew by in no time.

title_en: Pounding the Pavement More Than Anyone
excerpt_en: It's Saturday, so I had a walking event in the morning! 11 people participated today. Some regulars were there, and since I personally find it boring to walk the same route every time, this time I went for it and tried walking to a park that's reachable on foot from the city center.
body_en:
It's Saturday,

so I had a walking event in the morning!

11 people participated today.

Some regulars were there,

and since I personally find it boring

to walk the same route every time,

this time I went for it and tried walking to a park that's reachable on foot from the city center.

This was a hit with the participants --

"I had no idea a place like this existed!

I'll come back here on my own sometime!"

they said!

Since I wander around Dublin all the time, I've got a pretty good grasp of the city center.

That's exactly why I can plan the routes.

Finding different roads each week, looking for relatively wide paths to walk, looping around the city in about an hour, and ending at a cafe -- it looks easy but it's actually pretty hard.

In my case, I'm doing it by feel already,

but today I managed to walk 4 kilometers

in exactly one hour.

Of course, during the walk,

it's all about talking!

I personally love this talking part.

The ability to create opportunities for one-on-one conversations

comes from my experience as a photographer,

and my life experiences so far

are paying off in the details.

It's precisely because I've challenged myself with all sorts of things

that the dots are connecting.

I've been doing events for over a month now,

so I'm no longer a newcomer.

I carry myself confidently

as the event organizer.

That's why

participants are surprised when I tell them

I've only been here for just over 2 months. Haha.

And so, this time too,

it was an incredibly fun event.

I should change up the group photo spot soon too,

so I need to find a new one! Haha.

After the walk,

we had a casual hangout at Starbucks!

There, I actively chatted with people I couldn't talk to during the walk.

Beyond improving my English,

the information exchange here lets me

learn about countries I'll visit in the future,

so it's treasure for me!

And there are also wonderful encounters.

Card Game Too!

Since the Sunday card game turnout

hasn't been great lately,

this time I tried holding a SILVA event on Saturday.

About 15 people had reserved,

but only 3 actually showed up.

This was unexpected,

but the trade-off was that the interaction with those who came was deep and meaningful.

This time it was an international exchange

between Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Turkey, and Japan.

Quite a diverse crew!

Everyone was really great,

and the person in front had also participated in the last SILVA event --

a repeat visitor!

I want to increase the number of people

who come back for SILVA going forward.

Philippines Work

After finishing the two events

I organized,

it was desk work time!

For the photo shoots I did in the Philippines from February to April this year, I sent messages to all the models to issue digital certificates for the photo book as NFTs.

Despite it being the middle of the night,

many of them replied,

and I was happy to confirm that FOMUS's philosophy

of "connecting through masu" is actually spreading,

and it was fun catching up after so long.

These encounters only exist because

I went there in person

and connected with people,

which makes it incredibly rewarding.

Birthday Party

In the evening, I went to a birthday party

for a Japanese university student

who had attended my walking event before.

Since they personally invited me,

I cleared my schedule

and joined.

Honestly, I had no idea who else would be there,

but when I went, I saw some familiar faces here and there.

On top of that, someone who had been at today's walking event was also there, which surprised me. Haha.

It was mostly Japanese people,

but there were also people from Ireland and Turkey,

and I got to chat with them about all sorts of things.

And one of the Irish guys

had apparently started kendo here,

which blew my mind.

I did kendo from elementary school through high school,

so I had no idea there was a kendo dojo in Dublin.

I've been away from kendo for about 10 years,

but trying it in Dublin could be kind of interesting,

I thought.

We got along well,

so I'm planning to go check it out

at some point.

These kinds of encounters

don't happen unless you go out there yourself,

which once again reminds me that taking action is important.

What I can do is

take more action than anyone and meet as many people as possible.

And increase the number of people who find FOMUS and myself interesting, and find allies.

For that, I'll fly around as much as it takes.

And so,

today was another day of running around non-stop!

Tomorrow there's another walking event.

The weather is unstable and there are about 20 reservations,

so the actual turnout will probably be low,

but I'll still put on the event with care!

excerpt_en: Today is Sunday, so following Saturday, it's walking event day. 17 people had reserved. I figured half would be a good turnout, and headed to the meeting spot. Typically, people gather between 5 minutes early to 15 minutes late.
body_en:
Today is Sunday,

so following Saturday,

it's walking event day.

17 people had reserved.

I figured half would be a good turnout,

and headed to the meeting spot.

Typically, people gather between

5 minutes early to 15 minutes late.

I waited at the meeting spot for everyone to arrive.

...

10 minutes passed, and nobody came.

...

Huh?? Wait, what??!

...

I shared the location and everything,

but nobody's coming?!

..

Yep.

For the first time since I started the events,

not a single person showed up!!

17 out of 17 -- no-shows with no notice!!

This is overseas quality for you!! Haha.

So,

I posted the raw situation as an unlisted video on YouTube.

https://youtu.be/mlHf7BsX_rw?si=XsWjvvsLI6O4TE_8

For the first time in my life, I experienced zero attendees at an event.

If it were Japanese people, they'd at least

send a message if they couldn't make it,

but this is Ireland.

And since people from various countries are hitting the RSVP button, I can't control this part.

To prevent this kind of situation,

having people pay in advance

is the sure way.

If I knew nobody was coming,

I could just cancel the event

from the start,

but if people make it normal to "RSVP" and then not show up, I can't gauge the headcount at all.

This is a real pain point.

Do I increase the total pool of participants

to increase the actual turnout,

or do I have confirmed participants

pay online in advance

and create some level of commitment

to make sure they come?

It's a tough call.

Over this past month,

I've been designing events

to be more efficient

and to increase satisfaction,

but I was focused on increasing satisfaction for people who actually attend,

and it was my mistake not to think about how to increase the actual show-up rate relative to the number of reservations.

I don't know if I'll find a solution right away,

but I'll try exploring various options.

Now, having written all that,

actually, after this,

one student from South Africa

came to see me 45 minutes late.

He was coming from a place over an hour by train from Dublin,

and his train was delayed, making him late.

We didn't do the walk,

but we chatted at a different venue.

I told him about FOMUS,

and he was really interested in masu.

In a way, he saved me.

Next time I have some free time,

I'm thinking of visiting his university!

Jump In and Win

After that, I had one online meeting,

and then headed straight

to the Japan meetup event.

At first glance, there seemed to be fewer Japanese people today.

Just a few acquaintances here and there.

Honestly, for a moment, I considered just going home

and doing desk work,

but since I was already there,

I decided to socialize!

So,

I went over to two Japanese women.

Both of them had just arrived about a week ago.

And what was personally great

was that they were here on working holiday visas.

That means they're planning to stay long-term.

What I've learned over these 10 weeks is

that if I'm going to spend my time,

I'd rather talk to people who'll be living here long-term

rather than those who are leaving soon,

so it was lucky that the people I happened to talk to

were on working holidays.

We chatted about all sorts of things,

and after the event, we went straight

to a pub for drinks.

The kind of people who choose a place like Ireland

tend to be interesting and unique,

and talking with them is just fun.

That's why

these kinds of connections are precious.

In the end, both in work and life, "people" matter most,

and the presence of people who are fun to talk to and who inspire you is huge.

No matter what, the things that make you grow are

"environment" and "people,"

and if you don't jump in yourself,

these encounters don't happen.

This time, it was because both the women and I

each jumped into the meetup event

that we were able to meet.

When I first arrived, there was a moment where I almost went home,

and I realized that somewhere in my mind, there was still a little bit of a "can't be bothered" feeling.

But once you're actually inside,

from that point, how you carry yourself

can change everything,

which reminds me once again how important it is.

It was an event where I learned a lot.

By the way, as a side note,

we started as a group of 3,

then 2 men joined.

The 2 men were a Japanese guy and an Irish guy.

Since questions tend to be directed at the women,

in these situations, I basically take the listener role.

How I act in a group of 3 versus a group of 5 --

I change my role depending on the setting.

The male interest tends to gravitate toward the women. Haha.

Questions naturally go to the women too.

Besides, I understand

my own strengths,

so there's no need to force my own stories in --

being a listener actually

creates more time for me to talk later.

This kind of awareness comes from

the experience I've built up in Japan and abroad,

and once I do share about myself,

I've got a ton of stories from my life experiences

that get people interested.

I just need to drop those stories at the right time

and calibrate.

The old me would have

forced the conversation to be about me, but now I've learned to understand the timing -- not out of timidity, but in a strategic sense. Haha.

Of course, this works among Japanese people, but when talking with people from overseas, you actually need to go on the offensive yourself or nothing happens. Haha.

The cultures are just totally different!

And so,

even though the walking event was cancelled,

one person showed up late

and I got to have a one-on-one conversation,

and the meetup afterward

led to some wonderful encounters,

so my life in Ireland is always full of ups and downs.

All in all, I'm having one heck of an interesting final autumn of my 20s. Haha.

And with that, it's on to Week 11!

title_en: More Things Go Wrong Than Right
excerpt_en: Week 11 of activities in Ireland is underway! I really feel how fast time passes these days. After language school ends, it's straight into FOMUS activities -- either desk work or online meetings. One way or another, I've been keeping busy.
body_en:
Week 11 of activities in Ireland is underway!

I really feel how fast time passes these days.

After language school ends, it's straight into FOMUS activities --

either desk work

or online meetings.

One way or another, I've been keeping busy.

Since it's now Week 11,

I figured I'd share what's not going well.

Honestly, I could list things forever.

- Spreading the FOMUS brand

- Event participation rates

- English improvement

- Masu Photo

- Interacting with Irish people

- House hunting

Spreading the FOMUS Brand

To be honest, I still have a long way to go.

I've only managed to place products in 2 stores so far,

and I haven't found any companies that want to use FOMUS masu.

The biggest issue is

that I haven't done enough outreach yet.

I need to get out there more.

Since coming to Ireland,

I haven't met any Japanese people doing serious business here, which is the reality.

How do I break into the village that is Dublin?

I haven't found the key person yet either.

My guess is that the people at big companies

in central Dublin are the ones

driving business,

so how do I become a "villager"?

And how do I even approach them in the first place?

I need to think about these things.

FOMUS is fundamentally about "masu,"

so more people need to know about it,

and while using social media,

I basically need to get out there

and meet more people myself.

Unlike Japan, communication

always requires "English,"

which is another barrier.

Event Participation Rates

The walking events and card game events

that I started in late August --

the algorithm boost from the Meetup app

has ended,

and participants are growing slowly.

However,

the number of people who actually show up is declining too.

The other day, 17 people reserved

and not a single one showed up,

and controlling that part is difficult.

On top of that, temperatures will drop further from here,

so fewer people will want to join

a walking event in the morning.

As for the card game events,

since you can play indoors, there's more potential.

Plus, the game we play is "SILVA," developed by FOMUS,

so if people come when I hold the events,

it directly promotes FOMUS.

I might be able to go deeper with this part.

English Improvement

As for my English,

honestly, I'm still at the "not nearly enough" level.

Regular one-on-one communication

isn't much of a problem,

but when it's a group conversation,

the difficulty goes up for sure.

I want to talk more,

but the words I want to say

just don't come out sometimes.

School ends in 2 weeks,

so after that, I'll have to find my own time to study. And I need to participate in more social events to practice conversation.

It might be hard to become fluent in just one year, but I'll grow as much as I possibly can.

Masu Photo

As for Masu Photo,

I'm currently recruiting models.

Finding models in Ireland

is surprisingly difficult, to be honest.

I haven't met any Irish people yet for this.

I can't make an Ireland photo book

without a single Irish person in it. Haha.

So,

I need to find Irish models somehow.

The Ireland edition of Masu Photo

doesn't need to be finished until next year,

so I'll carefully evaluate and recruit

and make offers for models.

Interacting with Irish People

This has been really lacking.

Besides my homestay family,

the number of Irish people I've talked to

can be counted on two hands.

Most people using the regular Meetup app are probably people from other countries living here,

not Irish locals.

Maybe I haven't met them because I haven't gone to local Irish pubs that aren't tourist-oriented.

Anyway, to connect with

interesting people doing interesting things

in Ireland,

I'll move as much as I can and find a breakthrough.

House Hunting

And lastly, house hunting.

Honestly, prices are high.

Finding a place in Dublin is incredibly difficult,

so good properties don't come along easily.

After graduating from school, I'll head straight to Estonia,

come back once for Halloween,

then travel around Malta and Greece,

and after returning, travel around Ireland,

and by late November or early December, I want to find a place

and make good use of my time there.

Also, I have many friends right now

who are also helping me look for a place.

It reminds me once again

how important these connections truly are.

And after school ends

and I'm back from my overseas trips around late November,

I want to be in a situation where a new home is already lined up.

So, I've been rambling on

about all the things that aren't going well.

It makes me realize once again

that I need to work even harder.

And the most important thing is

finding a way into Dublin's inner circle!

I'll keep pushing and trying!
